_meta.purpose: Per-state concrete data dictionary for Phase A.2 state-vertical hub pages (residential driveway / patio / slab pour focus)
_meta.sources: ACI 318-19 + ACI 332 Residential Code; USDA-NRCS state frost depth maps; ASCE 7-22 seismic design category; state-specific expansive soil disclosures (TX, CO, OK); ASTM C150 Type I/II cement specifications; state contractor licensing boards; BLS OEWS May 2024 (SOC 47-2051 Cement Masons & Concrete Finishers state means); ACI 201.2R-16 freeze-thaw exposure classification.

fields.frost_line_inches: USDA-NRCS design frost depth (inches) — drives slab thickness, footing depth, gravel base spec
fields.freeze_thaw_exposure: F0 / F1 / F2 / F3 — ACI 201.2R freeze-thaw exposure category (drives air-entrainment %)
fields.typical_psi: ASTM/ACI residential driveway and patio prescriptive minimum (3000 / 3500 / 4000 / 4500 psi)
fields.expansive_soil_risk: very-high / high / moderate / low / none — soil shrink-swell index driving foundation design
fields.sulfate_exposure_class: S0 / S1 / S2 / S3 — ACI 318 sulfate exposure category (drives Type II or V cement)
fields.seismic_design_category: A / B / C / D0 / D1 / D2 / E / F — ASCE 7-22 SDC (drives rebar tie schedule + footing reinforcement)
fields.salt_de_icer_exposure: very-high / high / moderate / low / none — winter de-icing salt + chloride exposure on slab edges
fields.hurricane_tier: HVHZ / wind_high / wind_moderate / wind_low / none — coastal hurricane wind tier driving foundation tie-down
fields.license_board_name: Issuing authority for residential concrete contractor licensing
fields.permit_typical: Typical permit threshold for residential driveway / patio pour
fields.cement_mason_wage_mean_hourly: BLS OEWS May 2024 state mean hourly wage for SOC 47-2051 Cement Masons & Concrete Finishers (USD)
fields.avg_driveway_low: Typical low end of 600 sq ft 4-inch concrete driveway pour (USD)
fields.avg_driveway_high: Typical high end (decorative stamped, integrally-colored, or post-tension foundation) (USD)
fields.distinctive_law: Single most distinctive state-specific concrete, foundation, soil disclosure, or seismic rule
fields.climate_concern: Primary climate, soil, or load driver of concrete mix design and slab spec in this state

AL.name: Alabama
AL.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for driveway > 200 sq ft on new construction; HBLB residential license required above $10,000 contract
AL.distinctive_law: Alabama Code §34-14A-5 makes the Alabama Home Builders Licensure Board the only state body that licenses residential concrete contractors above the $10,000 contract threshold — and the HBLB requires a Limited Specialty Construction credential with a separate examination on flatwork, footings, and post-tension foundation work, plus Alabama is one of the only Southeast states requiring a separate Alabama Department of Environmental Management Stormwater NOI on every concrete pour above 1 acre disturbed area for residential subdivisions.
AL.climate_concern: Alabama's combination of red-clay subsoil across Birmingham, Tuscaloosa, and Montgomery requires aggressive 4-to-6-inch crushed-stone gravel base under every driveway pour to prevent clay-driven slab heave during seasonal moisture cycles — and Mobile-Baldwin coastal humidity drives rapid set times on every Gulf Coast pour, with retarder admixtures standard on every Alabama coastal residential pour during May-September high-humidity season.

AK.name: Alaska
AK.license_board_name: Alaska Department of Commerce — Construction Contractors Section
AK.permit_typical: borough building permit required for residential concrete pour; Construction Contractor with Residential Endorsement required
AK.distinctive_law: Alaska AS 18.65.082 requires every Alaska concrete contractor to hold a Construction Contractor with Residential Endorsement plus carry a $20,000 surety bond — the highest residential bond floor of any U.S. concrete contractor licensing scheme — and Alaska AS 30.05 separately requires Anchorage and Fairbanks municipalities to enforce ASCE 7-22 SDC D2 seismic design category footing reinforcement schedules on every residential foundation pour, the most restrictive seismic requirement in the country alongside Southern California.
AK.climate_concern: Alaska's combination of 100-inch frost depth in interior climate zones 7-8, F3 (most severe) freeze-thaw exposure category, and -50°F winter low temperatures forces every Alaska residential concrete pour to specify 6%+ air entrainment, Type II low-alkali cement, and minimum 4500 psi compressive strength — and Anchorage and Mat-Su Borough seismic design category D2 plus Aleutian Chain D2 design forces aggressive rebar tie schedules and continuous footing reinforcement on every residential foundation pour statewide.

AZ.name: Arizona
AZ.license_board_name: Arizona Registrar of Contractors — license CR-9 (Concrete) or CR-78 (Swimming Pools w/ concrete)
AZ.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; ROC CR-9 specialty license required on contracts above $1,000
AZ.distinctive_law: Arizona Registrar of Contractors §32-1121 sets one of the lowest residential contracting thresholds in the country at $1,000 — every Arizona concrete contractor exceeding that figure must hold a CR-9 Concrete specialty license, and Arizona is one of the only Mountain West states with mandatory ASTM C150 Type II/V sulfate-resistant cement on every residential pour due to high alkaline soil sulfate exposure across the Phoenix and Tucson basins, with Arizona Department of Environmental Quality additionally requiring SWPPP notification on every concrete pour above 1 acre disturbed area.
AZ.climate_concern: Arizona's combination of high alkaline soil sulfate exposure (S2 ACI 318 category) across the Phoenix, Tucson, and Mesa basins forces ASTM C150 Type II or Type V sulfate-resistant cement specification on every residential pour to prevent sulfate-driven concrete deterioration — and Arizona's expansive caliche-clay subsoil across the Sonoran Desert requires 4-to-6-inch crushed-aggregate base plus geotextile membrane separation on every Phoenix and Tucson driveway pour to prevent clay-driven slab heave during monsoon-season moisture cycles.

AR.name: Arkansas
AR.license_board_name: Arkansas Contractors Licensing Board — Residential Remodeler (>$2,000)
AR.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour on new construction; residential remodeler license required above $2,000 contract
AR.distinctive_law: Arkansas Code §17-25-101 sets one of the lowest residential contracting thresholds at $2,000 — and Arkansas is unusual among Southern states in adopting the New Madrid Seismic Zone Special Inspection Program through Arkansas Code §12-30 requiring continuous-rebar-tie footings and minimum 3500 psi compressive strength on every residential foundation pour in Crittenden, Greene, Mississippi, Poinsett, and other northeast Arkansas counties within the New Madrid Seismic Zone influence area.
AR.climate_concern: Arkansas's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across northeast Arkansas counties from Jonesboro to West Memphis forces ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in the corridor — and Arkansas's red-clay subsoil across the Delta and Western Arkansas requires aggressive 4-to-6-inch crushed-stone gravel base under every driveway pour, plus Ozark and Ouachita mountain pours additionally require frost-protected footing depth on every elevated residential construction site above 1,500 ft elevation.

CA.name: California
CA.license_board_name: California Contractors State License Board — C-8 (Concrete) or C-50 (Reinforcing Steel)
CA.permit_typical: city building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; CSLB C-8 license required above $500 contract; mandatory City/County engineered drainage plan in coastal California
CA.distinctive_law: California Title 24 Part 9 (California Building Code) plus ASCE 7-22 SDC D2 seismic design category requires the most aggressive residential concrete reinforcement scheme in the country — every California residential foundation pour requires continuous-rebar-tie schedules with minimum #4 rebar at 16-inch on-center plus continuous bond beam reinforcement, and California Title 24 Part 11 (CALGreen) additionally requires SCM-blended concrete (Type IL or Type IS) on every covered residential project, with CDPH-certified concrete admixture compliance reviewed on every Bay Area, Los Angeles, and San Diego residential pour.
CA.climate_concern: California's combination of ASCE 7-22 SDC D2 seismic design category across the entire state, expansive Bay Area and Central Valley clay subsoil, and Pacific salt-spray on every coastal exposure drives the most expensive residential concrete pour requirements in the country — and California's CAL FIRE Tier 3 WUI overlay across the Sierra foothills additionally requires Class A fire-rated concrete fascia and continuous concrete walkway separation on every WUI residential property to provide ember-resistant foundation perimeter protection.

CO.name: Colorado
CO.license_board_name: No statewide concrete license — Denver, Colorado Springs, Aurora issue municipal contractor credentials
CO.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 100 sq ft; mandatory soils report disclosure required on every Colorado residential foundation pour above 5% slope per CO Soil Disclosure Act
CO.distinctive_law: Colorado Soil Disclosure Act (CRS §6-6.5) imposes the most restrictive expansive-soil disclosure regime in the country — every Colorado residential property seller must provide a written disclosure of swelling soil risk to every buyer, and every Colorado residential foundation pour must include a Colorado Geological Survey-recognized soils report identifying expansive bentonitic clay subsoil risk, plus Colorado Front Range subdivisions routinely require post-tension foundation specifications instead of standard slab-on-grade due to bentonite-driven slab heave.
CO.climate_concern: Colorado's combination of expansive bentonitic clay subsoil across the Front Range and Eastern Plains, F2 (severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the state, and 36-inch frost depth forces aggressive expansive-soil mitigation on every Colorado residential foundation pour — Front Range subdivisions in Aurora, Castle Rock, and Highlands Ranch routinely use post-tension foundation specifications instead of standard slab-on-grade, and Colorado road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge drives 6-7% air-entrainment specification on every residential pour to prevent freeze-thaw spalling.

CT.name: Connecticut
CT.license_board_name: Connecticut Department of Consumer Protection — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration
CT.permit_typical: city or town building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; HIC registration required above $200 contract
CT.distinctive_law: Connecticut General Statutes §20-419 creates the lowest-threshold mandatory Home Improvement Contractor registration in the country at $200 contract value — every Connecticut residential concrete contractor exceeding that figure must hold a HIC registration, with the DCP Guaranty Fund compensating homeowners up to $25,000 per project for HIC-contractor abandonment, and Connecticut additionally enforces the Connecticut Crumbling Foundation Law (CT Gen. Stat. §16a-44a) restricting pyrrhotite-contaminated aggregate on every residential foundation pour in 41 affected northeastern Connecticut towns.
CT.climate_concern: Connecticut's pyrrhotite-contaminated aggregate crisis affecting 41 northeastern Connecticut towns is the most acute residential concrete crisis in the country — Connecticut Department of Housing has cataloged over 30,000 affected homes with crumbling foundations from pyrrhotite-bearing aggregate sourced from a single quarry between 1983-2015, and every modern Connecticut residential foundation pour now requires aggregate source certification under the Connecticut Crumbling Foundation Law plus mandatory testing on suspect-source aggregate to prevent further pyrrhotite-driven foundation failure.

DE.name: Delaware
DE.license_board_name: Delaware Division of Revenue — Business License (concrete contractor)
DE.permit_typical: county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; Delaware Business License only
DE.distinctive_law: Delaware is one of the few East Coast states with no specialty concrete or building contractor licensing — every Delaware concrete contractor operates under a Delaware Division of Revenue Business License only, with no examination, bond, or board oversight, leaving Sussex County coastal property owners to rely on contractor BBB ratings and Delaware Consumer Protection Unit complaint history rather than a state credential check, plus Delaware Coastal Zone Act §7001 separately restricts concrete pour stormwater runoff within the Inland Bays watershed across Sussex County.
DE.climate_concern: Delaware's combination of Atlantic coastal salt-spray on every Sussex County coastal exposure, sandy fast-draining soils across the Cape Henlopen-to-Bethany corridor, and F1 freeze-thaw exposure forces standard 5% air-entrainment specification on every coastal Delaware residential pour — and Delaware Bay coastal salt-spray plus winter de-icing salt application on every grade-level driveway edge requires 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every Sussex County beach-side residential pour to manage chloride penetration on every concrete-driveway-replacement pour.

FL.name: Florida
FL.license_board_name: Florida Department of Business & Professional Regulation — Construction Industry Licensing Board (Certified or Registered Specialty Concrete Contractor)
FL.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential concrete pour; FL DBPR specialty license required statewide; HVHZ permit and Product Approval verification mandatory in Miami-Dade and Broward
FL.distinctive_law: Florida Building Code §1804 imposes the most restrictive coastal hurricane-zone foundation requirements in the country — every HVHZ residential concrete pour in Miami-Dade and Broward counties requires Florida Product Approval (FL #) compliance with Notice of Acceptance (NOA) testing for hurricane wind uplift up to 175 mph, plus continuous epoxy-coated rebar tie schedules and minimum 4500 psi compressive strength on hurricane-zone residential foundations, and Florida Statute §501.140 (Sinkhole Disclosure) separately requires every Florida property seller to disclose any sinkhole activity on every residential property transfer.
FL.climate_concern: Florida's combination of Atlantic and Gulf hurricane wind uplift requirements driving HVHZ Miami-Dade and Broward NOA-rated foundation requirements, persistent year-round humidity driving rapid concrete set times requiring retarder admixtures on every pour, and Florida's high water table across the entire peninsula requiring waterproof admixtures on every residential basement and below-grade pour drives the most specialized residential concrete mix design requirements in the Southeast.

GA.name: Georgia
GA.license_board_name: Georgia State Licensing Board for Residential and General Contractors
GA.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour on new construction; residential-basic or residential-light contractor license required above $2,500 contract
GA.distinctive_law: Georgia Code §43-41 created the State Licensing Board for Residential and General Contractors in 2008, making Georgia one of the most recent Southeast states to centralize residential contractor licensing — every Georgia concrete contractor exceeding the $2,500 contract threshold must hold a residential-basic or residential-light license, plus Georgia Code §44-3 separately enforces the Georgia Property Owners Association Act requiring HOA-approved concrete-driveway-replacement contractor selection on every covered residential property in deed-restricted communities, the most restrictive HOA concrete-pour regulation in the Southeast.
GA.climate_concern: Georgia's red-clay subsoil across the Atlanta Piedmont from Charlotte to Augusta requires aggressive 4-to-6-inch crushed-stone gravel base under every driveway pour to prevent clay-driven slab heave during seasonal moisture cycles — and Georgia's high mature-deciduous tree canopy across the Atlanta-Athens corridor combined with persistent root-driven slab uplift on existing driveways drives standard root-barrier and continuous expansion-joint installation on every Atlanta and Athens residential driveway replacement pour.

HI.name: Hawaii
HI.license_board_name: Hawaii Department of Commerce & Consumer Affairs — Contractors License Board (C-31 Concrete)
HI.permit_typical: county building permit required for residential concrete pour; C-31 specialty license required statewide on contracts above $1,500
HI.distinctive_law: Hawaii HRS §444 requires every Hawaii concrete contractor to hold a Contractors License Board C-31 Concrete specialty license — Hawaii is unusual in housing volcanic-ash-aggregate concrete supply chains across the state, with Hawaii Department of Health Materials Testing Program enforcing aggregate-source certification on every residential pour to prevent volcanic-ash-driven alkali-aggregate reaction (AAR), plus Hawaii Building Code §1704 requires continuous Special Inspector observation on every Class B foundation pour due to ASCE 7-22 SDC D1 seismic design category statewide.
HI.climate_concern: Hawaii's combination of ASCE 7-22 SDC D1 seismic design category across all four major Hawaiian islands, Pacific salt-spray on every windward coastal exposure, and the most expensive concrete supply chain in the country (Type II cement imported from California and Asia) drives Hawaii residential concrete pour costs 80-150% higher than mainland averages — and Hawaii's volcanic-ash-aggregate supply requires aggregate-source certification on every residential pour to prevent alkali-aggregate reaction (AAR) driven concrete deterioration.

ID.name: Idaho
ID.license_board_name: Idaho Division of Occupational and Professional Licenses — Contractor Registration
ID.permit_typical: city building permit required for residential driveway pour on new construction; Idaho Contractor Registration required statewide
ID.distinctive_law: Idaho Code §54-5210 established the Idaho Contractor Registration program in 2005 as a no-exam registration scheme — every Idaho concrete contractor must register annually with the Division of Occupational and Professional Licenses, but unlike most state systems Idaho imposes no examination, no surety bond requirement, and no residential recovery fund, leaving Boise, Idaho Falls, and Coeur d'Alene homeowners with only the Idaho Attorney General Consumer Protection Division as a recovery channel, plus Idaho Code §22-3401 grants the Idaho State Department of Agriculture exclusive authority over residential pesticide regulation affecting concrete-pour soil-prep sequencing.
ID.climate_concern: Idaho's combination of high-altitude UV exposure across the Wood River Valley and Boise foothills, F2 (severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the entire state, and 36-inch frost depth forces 6%+ air-entrainment specification plus Type II cement on every Idaho residential pour — and Boise, Idaho Falls, and Twin Falls valley smoke from regional summer wildfire seasons since 2017 has measurably stressed fresh concrete cure cycles requiring extended wet-curing periods on every Idaho residential pour during August-October wildfire season.

IL.name: Illinois
IL.license_board_name: No statewide concrete license — Chicago Department of Buildings issues municipal credentials
IL.permit_typical: city building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; Chicago Class B General Contractor registration required for any city residential concrete contract above $5,000
IL.distinctive_law: Illinois Prevailing Wage Act (820 ILCS 130) makes every Cook County residential concrete contractor subject to prevailing wage requirements on any project receiving Tax Increment Financing or other municipal subsidies — Illinois prevailing wage rates for cement masons in Chicago metro routinely exceed $45/hr base + $25/hr fringe (the highest residential cement mason rate in the country), and Illinois Code 105 ILCS 5/1A separately requires PRP-class flatwork on every residential property within 1/2 mile of an Illinois public school as a defined-school-zone safety mandate.
IL.climate_concern: Illinois's combination of 42-inch frost depth across the Chicagoland-Rockford-Peoria corridor, F2 (severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the entire state, and Illinois road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge from Illinois Department of Transportation snow operations forces 6%+ air-entrainment plus 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every residential pour — and Illinois's pre-1900 housing-stock concentration in Chicago bungalow belt, Rockford, and Peoria requires careful subgrade prep and pyrrhotite-aggregate testing on every concrete-driveway-replacement pour.

IN.name: Indiana
IN.license_board_name: No statewide concrete license — Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, Evansville issue municipal contractor credentials
IN.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; Indianapolis Class A Improvement Contractor registration required for any city contract above $5,000
IN.distinctive_law: Indiana Code §22-12 grants the Indiana Department of Homeland Security Fire Prevention & Building Safety Commission statewide building code authority but no concrete-specific contractor licensing — Indiana operates under the Indiana Residential Code 2020 (based on IRC 2018) with municipal contractor registration in Indianapolis, Fort Wayne, Evansville, and South Bend providing the only operational concrete contractor regulatory channel, plus Indiana's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across southwestern Indiana counties from Vincennes to Evansville triggers ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 design category requirements on every residential foundation pour.
IN.climate_concern: Indiana's combination of 36-inch frost depth across the entire state, F2 (severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the central-Midwest, and Indiana road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge from Indiana Department of Transportation snow operations forces 6%+ air-entrainment plus 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every residential pour — and Indiana's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across Posey, Vanderburgh, Warrick, Spencer, and Perry counties triggers ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in southwestern Indiana.

IA.name: Iowa
IA.license_board_name: Iowa Workforce Development — Construction Contractor Registration
IA.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; Iowa Construction Contractor Registration required statewide above $2,000 annual gross
IA.distinctive_law: Iowa Code §91C requires every concrete contractor performing more than $2,000 in annual gross construction work in Iowa to register with Iowa Workforce Development — Iowa is unusual in tying contractor registration to annual gross rather than per-project value, plus Iowa Department of Transportation Specifications 2412 enforces statewide aggregate-source certification on every concrete pour for ASR (alkali-silica reaction) and ACR (alkali-carbonate reaction) prevention, with Iowa State University Construction Materials Lab providing mandatory Class C aggregate testing on every residential foundation pour above 1,000 sq ft.
IA.climate_concern: Iowa's combination of 48-inch frost depth across the entire state, F2 (severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the central-Midwest, and -25°F winter low temperatures forces 6%+ air-entrainment plus Type II cement on every Iowa residential pour — and Iowa road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge from Iowa Department of Transportation snow operations drives 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every Iowa residential pour to manage chloride penetration on every concrete-driveway-replacement pour.

KS.name: Kansas
KS.license_board_name: No statewide concrete license — Wichita, Overland Park, Kansas City KS issue municipal contractor credentials
KS.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 100 sq ft; municipal contractor registration required in Wichita and Overland Park
KS.distinctive_law: Kansas has no state-level contractor licensing for concrete or general residential building work — Kansas Statute §75-2935 reserves all construction trade regulation to municipalities, leaving Kansas one of only four states with zero state credential requirement for residential concrete contracting, and forcing Wichita Metropolitan Building Standards, Overland Park Community Development, and Kansas City KS Code Enforcement to operate independent contractor registration schedules with no reciprocity across city boundaries plus Kansas Department of Health & Environment additionally requiring SWPPP notification on residential pours above 1 acre disturbed area.
KS.climate_concern: Kansas's expansive Permian-aged clay subsoil across central Kansas from Wichita to Salina forces ASTM C150 Type II sulfate-resistant cement specification on every residential pour to prevent sulfate-driven concrete deterioration — and central-plains hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely damage fresh concrete cure cycles during the April-June severe weather season, requiring careful pour scheduling around forecast hail events plus protective sheeting on every Kansas residential pour during severe weather alerts.

KY.name: Kentucky
KY.license_board_name: No statewide concrete license — Louisville Metro and Lexington-Fayette Urban County issue municipal contractor credentials
KY.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; Louisville Metro contractor registration required for residential exterior contracting above $5,000
KY.distinctive_law: Kentucky has no statewide residential concrete contractor licensing — Louisville Metro Department of Codes & Regulations and Lexington-Fayette Urban County Government Building Inspection each operate independent contractor registration schedules, and Kentucky's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across the Jackson Purchase region from Paducah to Mayfield triggers ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in westernmost Kentucky counties plus the Kentucky Building Code §1613 separately requires Special Inspector continuous observation on every Class B foundation pour in seismic zone counties.
KY.climate_concern: Kentucky's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across the Jackson Purchase region from Paducah to Mayfield triggers ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in westernmost Kentucky counties — and Kentucky's karst topography with limestone-driven sinkhole risk across central Kentucky from Louisville to Bowling Green requires geotechnical investigation on every Kentucky residential foundation pour to identify subsurface void or solution cavity risk, plus Eastern Kentucky Appalachian hill-country pours additionally require terraced retaining wall design on every sloping residential lot.

LA.name: Louisiana
LA.license_board_name: Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors — Residential Building Contractor or Specialty
LA.permit_typical: city or parish building permit required for residential concrete pour; LSLBC residential license required above $7,500 contract; coastal hurricane wind-zone post-tension foundation requirements
LA.distinctive_law: Louisiana RS §37:2156 requires every Louisiana concrete contractor exceeding the $7,500 residential threshold to hold a Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors residential license — and Louisiana Building Code §1804 separately requires post-tension foundation specifications on every residential foundation pour in alluvial South Louisiana parishes from Baton Rouge to New Orleans due to highly variable Mississippi River Delta soil conditions, with LSLBC additionally enforcing Louisiana Code §38:2212 statewide concrete-supplier certification on every residential pour above 5 cu yd to prevent fly-ash and slag substitution.
LA.climate_concern: Louisiana's combination of alluvial Mississippi River Delta soils across South Louisiana from Baton Rouge to New Orleans, persistent year-round humidity driving rapid concrete set times requiring retarder admixtures, and hurricane wind uplift requirements force post-tension foundation specifications on the majority of Louisiana residential foundation pours — and Louisiana's high water table across the entire state requires waterproof admixtures on every residential basement and below-grade pour, plus continuous epoxy-coated rebar tie schedules on every coastal Louisiana foundation pour to manage Gulf-driven chloride penetration.

ME.name: Maine
ME.license_board_name: No statewide concrete license; Maine Construction Wage Bureau enforces wage compliance on residential pours
ME.permit_typical: town building permit required for residential concrete pour > 100 sq ft
ME.distinctive_law: Maine has no state-level contractor licensing for concrete or general residential building work — and Maine Statute §22 MRS §1322 requires careful pyrrhotite-aggregate testing on every residential foundation pour given Maine's geological proximity to the Connecticut pyrrhotite-contamination zone, with Maine Geological Survey providing mandatory aggregate-source certification on every Maine quarry-supplied residential pour to prevent further pyrrhotite-driven foundation failure spread from northeastern Connecticut into southern Maine.
ME.climate_concern: Maine's combination of 60-inch frost depth in interior climate zones 6A-7, F3 (most severe) freeze-thaw exposure category, and -30°F winter low temperatures forces 6%+ air-entrainment plus Type II cement on every Maine residential pour — and Maine's coastal salt-spray on Penobscot Bay, Casco Bay, and Mount Desert Island residential properties combined with Maine road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge drives 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio plus continuous epoxy-coated rebar tie schedules on every coastal Maine residential pour.

MD.name: Maryland
MD.license_board_name: Maryland Home Improvement Commission (MHIC) — Home Improvement Contractor
MD.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; MHIC license required above $500 contract
MD.distinctive_law: Maryland Business Regulation §8-301 requires every Maryland concrete contractor exceeding the $500 residential threshold to hold a Maryland Home Improvement Commission (MHIC) Home Improvement Contractor license — and Maryland's Chesapeake Bay Critical Area Act (Annotated Code §8-1801) imposes the most restrictive coastal stormwater management framework in the country requiring continuous concrete impervious-surface review on every residential pour within 1,000 ft of tidal waters, doubling typical Maryland coastal residential pour permit timelines and adding mandatory bioretention infiltration design on every covered project.
MD.climate_concern: Maryland's combination of Chesapeake Bay Critical Area Act stormwater requirements within 1,000 ft of tidal waters, Maryland road de-icing salt exposure from Maryland Department of Transportation snow operations, and 30-inch frost depth drives 6%+ air-entrainment plus 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every Maryland residential pour — and Maryland's pre-1978 housing-stock concentration in Baltimore rowhouse districts requires specialty narrow-cavity concrete-driveway-replacement contractor specialization to manage existing rowhouse foundation tie-ins.

MA.name: Massachusetts
MA.license_board_name: Massachusetts Office of Consumer Affairs — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) registration
MA.permit_typical: city or town building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; HIC registration required above $1,000 contract
MA.distinctive_law: Massachusetts MGL Chapter 142A imposes the most restrictive Home Improvement Contractor framework in the Northeast — every Massachusetts concrete contractor exceeding the $1,000 contract threshold must hold a HIC registration with the Massachusetts Office of Consumer Affairs, plus the Massachusetts HIC Guaranty Fund compensates homeowners up to $10,000 per project for HIC-contractor breach on residential concrete contracts, and Massachusetts 780 CMR Stretch Code separately imposes Above-Code SCM-blended concrete (Type IL or Type IS) requirements on every covered residential project in 300+ Stretch Code-adopting municipalities.
MA.climate_concern: Massachusetts's combination of 48-inch frost depth in interior climate zones 5A-6A, F3 (most severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the entire state, and the highest pre-1900 wood-frame housing-stock per capita in the country drives the most expensive average residential concrete pour cycle in New England — Boston, Cambridge, Worcester, and Springfield pre-1900 historic-district concrete-driveway-replacement pours require careful preservation of original granite curbing and cobblestone aprons typical of Massachusetts pre-1920 housing.

MI.name: Michigan
MI.license_board_name: Michigan Department of Licensing and Regulatory Affairs — Residential Builder or Maintenance & Alteration Contractor (Concrete specialty)
MI.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; Michigan M&A Contractor (Concrete) license required above $600 contract
MI.distinctive_law: Michigan PA 299 of 1980 created the Maintenance & Alteration Contractor (Concrete) specialty license — Michigan is one of only a handful of states with concrete as its own specialty trade license rather than rolled up under general residential building, plus Michigan Public Service Commission's Detroit Lake aggregate-source restrictions under Michigan Aggregate Industries Permit Program limit certain Detroit-area aggregate sources from residential pours due to historical alkali-aggregate reaction (AAR) issues affecting pre-1990 Michigan residential foundations.
MI.climate_concern: Michigan's combination of 42-inch frost depth across the Lower Peninsula, F2 (severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the entire state, and Michigan road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge from Michigan Department of Transportation snow operations forces 6%+ air-entrainment plus 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every residential pour — and Michigan's lake-effect snow loads on Detroit, Grand Rapids, Traverse City, and Marquette landscapes drive minimum 4-inch slab thickness specifications plus continuous wire mesh reinforcement on every residential driveway pour.

MN.name: Minnesota
MN.license_board_name: Minnesota Department of Labor & Industry — Residential Building Contractor or Specialty
MN.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; DLI residential contractor license required above $15,000 annual gross
MN.distinctive_law: Minnesota Statute §326B requires every Minnesota concrete contractor performing more than $15,000 in annual gross residential construction work to hold a Minnesota Department of Labor & Industry Residential Building Contractor or Concrete Specialty license — plus Minnesota's Concrete Reinforcing Steel Institute (CRSI) Hot Weather and Cold Weather Concrete Specifications (incorporated into MN Building Code) require continuous concrete-cure-temperature monitoring on every residential pour during November-March cold-weather window, the most restrictive cold-weather concrete pour regime in the country.
MN.climate_concern: Minnesota's combination of 60-inch frost depth in interior climate zones 6A-7, F3 (most severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the entire state, and -40°F winter low temperatures drives the most aggressive residential concrete cold-weather pour requirements in the Lower 48 — and Minnesota road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge from Minnesota Department of Transportation snow operations plus Twin Cities lake-aerosol from 10,000+ regional lakes drives 6%+ air-entrainment plus 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every residential pour.

MS.name: Mississippi
MS.license_board_name: Mississippi State Board of Contractors — Residential Builder or Specialty
MS.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential concrete pour; MSBC residential license required above $50,000 contract or $10,000 in annual gross
MS.distinctive_law: Mississippi Code §31-3-15 sets one of the highest residential contracting thresholds at $50,000 per project — Mississippi State Board of Contractors only requires a Residential Builder license above that figure, leaving most Mississippi residential concrete contractors operating below the threshold without state credentials, and Mississippi's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across northwestern Mississippi counties from Tunica to Greenville triggers ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in the corridor.
MS.climate_concern: Mississippi's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across northwestern Mississippi Delta counties forces ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in the corridor — and Mississippi's expansive Yazoo Delta clay subsoil across the Mississippi Delta from Greenville to Vicksburg requires aggressive 4-to-6-inch crushed-stone gravel base under every driveway pour, plus Gulf Coast hurricane wind uplift requirements drive continuous epoxy-coated rebar tie schedules on every coastal Mississippi residential foundation pour.

MO.name: Missouri
MO.license_board_name: No statewide concrete license — St. Louis, Kansas City MO, Springfield issue municipal contractor credentials
MO.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; St. Louis Class B contractor registration required for residential exterior contracting above $5,000
MO.distinctive_law: Missouri has no statewide residential concrete contractor licensing — Missouri Revised Statutes §700 reserves construction trade regulation to municipalities, and Missouri's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across the Bootheel from Cape Girardeau to Hayti triggers ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in the corridor, plus Missouri Geological Survey provides mandatory karst-cavity hazard mapping on every Missouri residential foundation pour above 1,500 sq ft due to karst-driven sinkhole risk across central Missouri from Springfield to Columbia.
MO.climate_concern: Missouri's New Madrid Seismic Zone influence across the Bootheel triggers ASCE 7-22 SDC C-D0 seismic design category requirements on every residential foundation pour in the corridor — and Missouri's karst topography with limestone-driven sinkhole risk across central Missouri from Springfield to Columbia requires geotechnical investigation on every Missouri residential foundation pour to identify subsurface void or solution cavity risk, plus Missouri road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge from Missouri Department of Transportation snow operations drives 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio.

MT.name: Montana
MT.license_board_name: Montana Department of Labor & Industry — Independent Contractor Registration
MT.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; Montana Independent Contractor Registration required statewide
MT.distinctive_law: Montana Code §39-71-417 created the Montana Independent Contractor Registration program in 1987 as a workers'-compensation-focused registration — every Montana concrete contractor must register annually with Montana DLI but the program imposes no examination, no surety bond, and no recovery fund, focusing exclusively on workers'-compensation election and IRS contractor-status documentation, plus Montana's high alkaline soil sulfate exposure across central and eastern Montana forces ASTM C150 Type II/V sulfate-resistant cement specification on every residential pour to prevent sulfate-driven concrete deterioration.
MT.climate_concern: Montana's combination of high alkaline soil sulfate exposure (S2 ACI 318 category) across central and eastern Montana, F3 (most severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the entire state, and 48-inch frost depth forces Type II/V sulfate-resistant cement plus 6%+ air-entrainment specification on every Montana residential pour — and Bozeman, Missoula, and Helena valley smoke from regional summer wildfire seasons since 2017 has measurably stressed fresh concrete cure cycles requiring extended wet-curing periods on every Montana residential pour during August-October wildfire season.

NE.name: Nebraska
NE.license_board_name: Nebraska Department of Labor — Contractor Registration
NE.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; Nebraska Contractor Registration required statewide above $2,500 annual gross
NE.distinctive_law: Nebraska Revised Statute §48-2103 requires every Nebraska concrete contractor performing more than $2,500 in annual gross construction work to register with the Nebraska Department of Labor — Nebraska is one of the lowest-threshold registration states in the country, and Nebraska Department of Roads Specifications 503 requires statewide aggregate-source certification on every concrete pour for ASR (alkali-silica reaction) prevention given Nebraska's high-reactivity central-plains aggregate deposits, with University of Nebraska-Lincoln Construction Materials Lab providing mandatory aggregate testing on every residential foundation pour above 1,000 sq ft.
NE.climate_concern: Nebraska's combination of 42-inch frost depth across the entire state, F2 (severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the central-plains, and central-plains hailstorms with 2-inch+ diameter routinely damaging fresh concrete cure cycles drives 6%+ air-entrainment plus protective sheeting on every residential pour during severe weather alerts — and Nebraska road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge from Nebraska Department of Transportation snow operations plus Lincoln-Omaha winter chinook wind events drive 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every Nebraska residential pour.

NV.name: Nevada

NV.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; NSCB C-5 specialty license required statewide on contracts above $1,000
NV.distinctive_law: Nevada NRS §624 requires every Nevada concrete contractor exceeding the $1,000 residential threshold to hold a Nevada State Contractors Board C-5 Concrete specialty license — and Nevada's high alkaline soil sulfate exposure across the Las Vegas Valley combined with ASCE 7-22 SDC D0 seismic design category in Reno-Sparks-Carson City forces ASTM C150 Type II/V sulfate-resistant cement plus continuous-rebar-tie footings on every residential foundation pour in Northern Nevada, with the NSCB Residential Recovery Fund compensating Nevada homeowners up to $40,000 per single-family residence for licensed-contractor abandonment.
NV.climate_concern: Nevada's combination of high alkaline soil sulfate exposure (S2 ACI 318 category) across the Las Vegas Valley, ASCE 7-22 SDC D0 seismic design category in Reno-Sparks-Carson City, and extreme summer heat above 110°F driving rapid concrete set times forces ASTM C150 Type II/V sulfate-resistant cement plus retarder admixtures on every Nevada residential pour — and Las Vegas Valley afternoon thunderstorm season from July-September requires careful pour scheduling around forecast monsoon events plus protective sheeting on every Nevada residential pour during severe weather alerts.

NH.name: New Hampshire

NH.permit_typical: town building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft
NH.distinctive_law: New Hampshire has no statewide residential concrete contractor licensing — the Live Free or Die contractor regulation framework leaves general residential concrete unlicensed, and New Hampshire's geological proximity to the Connecticut pyrrhotite-contamination zone forces careful aggregate-source certification on every residential foundation pour given quarry-sourced aggregate supply chains crossing the Massachusetts-New Hampshire border, with New Hampshire Geological Survey providing mandatory aggregate-source review on every quarry-supplied New Hampshire residential foundation pour.
NH.climate_concern: New Hampshire's combination of 48-inch frost depth in interior climate zones 5A-6A, F3 (most severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the entire state, and -25°F winter low temperatures forces 6%+ air-entrainment plus Type II cement on every New Hampshire residential pour — and North Atlantic salt-spray on the Seacoast from Hampton to Portsmouth combined with New Hampshire road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge drives 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio plus continuous epoxy-coated rebar tie schedules on every coastal New Hampshire residential pour.

NJ.distinctive_law: New Jersey N.J.S.A. 56:8-136 (Contractors' Registration Act) requires every New Jersey concrete contractor exceeding the $500 residential threshold to register with the Division of Consumer Affairs as a Home Improvement Contractor — and New Jersey's pre-1978 housing-stock concentration in Newark, Jersey City, Paterson, and Elizabeth combined with persistent Atlantic coastal salt-spray on every Cape May, Atlantic, and Monmouth county exposure drives standard epoxy-coated rebar tie schedules on every coastal New Jersey residential foundation pour, plus New Jersey Coastal Area Facility Review Act (CAFRA) requires impervious-surface review on every residential pour within the 100-year flood zone.
NJ.climate_concern: New Jersey's combination of Atlantic coastal salt-spray on every Cape May, Atlantic, and Monmouth county exposure, persistent I-95 corridor humidity, and the highest pre-1978 housing-stock density in the Mid-Atlantic drives the most expensive average residential concrete pour cycle in the Garden State — Newark, Jersey City, Paterson, and Elizabeth concrete-driveway-replacement pours typically run 25-40% higher per-square-foot than national norms once HIC compliance, Jersey Shore marine-grade specifications, and pre-1978 historic-district concrete-driveway-replacement contractor specialization are factored into pricing.

WI.permit_typical: city or county building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; WI Dwelling Contractor Certification required for any one- and two-family dwelling exterior contracting
WI.distinctive_law: Wisconsin Statute §101.654 requires every Wisconsin concrete contractor performing exterior coating work on one- or two-family dwellings to hold a Wisconsin Department of Safety & Professional Services Dwelling Contractor Certification — and Wisconsin Department of Transportation Specifications 501 enforces statewide aggregate-source certification on every concrete pour for ASR (alkali-silica reaction) and ACR (alkali-carbonate reaction) prevention given Wisconsin Driftless Area and Door County high-reactivity dolomitic aggregate deposits, with University of Wisconsin Construction Materials Lab providing mandatory aggregate testing on every residential foundation pour above 1,000 sq ft.
WI.climate_concern: Wisconsin's combination of 48-inch frost depth across the entire state, F3 (most severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the Upper Midwest, and Lake Michigan and Lake Superior lake-effect humidity drives 6%+ air-entrainment plus Type II cement on every Wisconsin residential pour — and Wisconsin road de-icing salt exposure on every grade-level driveway edge from Wisconsin Department of Transportation snow operations plus winter ice-melt salt application on every residential driveway forces 0.40 maximum water-cement ratio on every Wisconsin residential pour.

WY.name: Wyoming
WY.license_board_name: No statewide concrete license; Cheyenne, Casper, Jackson issue municipal contractor credentials
WY.permit_typical: city building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; municipal contractor registration required in Cheyenne and Jackson
WY.distinctive_law: Wyoming has no state-level contractor licensing for concrete or general residential building work — Wyoming Statute §33-1 reserves all construction trade regulation to municipalities, leaving Wyoming one of only four states with zero state credential requirement for residential concrete contracting alongside Kansas, South Dakota, and Texas, and Wyoming's high alkaline soil sulfate exposure across the Powder River and Bighorn basins forces ASTM C150 Type V sulfate-resistant cement specification on every residential pour to prevent sulfate-driven concrete deterioration.
WY.climate_concern: Wyoming's combination of high alkaline soil sulfate exposure (S2 ACI 318 category) across the Powder River and Bighorn basins, F3 (most severe) freeze-thaw exposure across the entire state, and -35°F winter low temperatures forces ASTM C150 Type V sulfate-resistant cement plus 6%+ air-entrainment specification on every Wyoming residential pour — and Bighorn Basin and Powder River Basin oil-and-gas patch fugitive dust contamination plus regional summer wildfire-smoke seasons since 2017 measurably stress fresh concrete cure cycles requiring extended wet-curing periods on every Wyoming residential pour.

DC.name: District of Columbia
DC.license_board_name: DC Department of Licensing & Consumer Protection — Home Improvement Contractor (HIC) license
DC.permit_typical: DC building permit required for residential driveway pour > 200 sq ft; DC HIC license required above $300 contract
DC.distinctive_law: District of Columbia Municipal Regulation 16-800 requires every DC concrete contractor exceeding the $300 contract threshold to hold a Department of Licensing & Consumer Protection Home Improvement Contractor license — and DC Construction Codes Supplement (12-A DCMR §1801) imposes the most restrictive municipal-jurisdiction residential foundation pour requirements in the country including continuous Special Inspector observation on every residential foundation pour above 1,000 sq ft, plus the DC Department of Energy & Environment additionally requires Stormwater Management Plan review on every residential pour above 1,000 sq ft impervious area under the DC Stormwater Regulations.
DC.climate_concern: District of Columbia's combination of Chesapeake Bay watershed-protection nutrient caps under the DC Stormwater Regulations, mature deciduous canopy across Capitol Hill and Georgetown driving root-uplift risk on existing residential driveways, and 24-inch frost depth drives careful pre-pour root-barrier installation on every DC residential driveway-replacement pour — and DC's high concentration of pre-1900 brick rowhouse stock requires specialty narrow-cavity concrete-driveway-replacement contractor specialization to manage existing rowhouse foundation tie-ins on every covered residential pour.
